items.title: Greedy decoding
items.scenario: An LLM picks the highest-probability next token at each step.
items.explanation: Local choice. Fast and deterministic, but prone to getting stuck in repetitive or low-quality outputs; beam search and sampling are less greedy alternatives.

items.title: Top-k retrieval
items.scenario: Pick the k chunks with the highest similarity to the query for the prompt context.
items.explanation: Greedy ranking by score. Works because the local choice (pick the highest score) is the global definition of top-k.

items.title: Prompt token-budget allocation
items.scenario: You have 8k tokens for the prompt; system + history + retrieved chunks must fit.
items.explanation: Allocate to the highest-value section first (often retrieved chunks > history > examples) until the budget is exhausted — a greedy heuristic, not provably optimal but usually good enough.

items.title: Activity selection (classroom scheduling)
items.scenario: Pick the maximum non-overlapping set of meetings to fit in a shared room.
items.explanation: Sort by end time and greedily pick the next non-overlapping meeting — provably optimal.

items.title: Dijkstra shortest path
items.scenario: Shortest path in a knowledge graph weighted by relation cost (non-negative).
items.explanation: At each step, expand the frontier node with the smallest current cost — provably optimal because of the no-negative-edge assumption.
